hook: You already know how to work with numbers — but what happens when you have hundreds of them at once? Raw data on its own can be overwhelming and hard to interpret, which is exactly why mathematicians developed powerful tools to summarize and display it. In this unit, you'll discover how to turn messy, real-world data into clear pictures and meaningful statistics that tell a story at a glance.
concept: The unifying idea is that data becomes useful when we organize and summarize it — finding patterns, centers, and spreads that reveal what the numbers are really saying. This is the foundation of descriptive statistics.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to calculate and interpret the mean, median, and mode to find the single value that best represents an entire data set.
Master how to measure the spread of a data set using the range and interquartile range ($IQR = Q_3 - Q_1$) to understand how consistent or spread out your data is.
You will learn to construct and interpret box-and-whisker plots that visually display a data set's five-number summary, making it easy to spot the center, spread, and outliers.
Apply frequency distributions to build histograms that reveal the shape and pattern of large data sets grouped into intervals.
You will learn to organize numerical data into stem-and-leaf plots so you can see the distribution of values while preserving every individual data point.
Master two-way tables to organize and analyze relationships between two categorical variables, uncovering patterns that a simple list of data would hide.
You will learn to evaluate the strengths of each display type and confidently select the most effective way to visualize any given data set.
